bibliographic_data.title: Taming Fat-Tailed ("Heavier-Tailed'' with Potentially Infinite Variance) Noise in Federated Learning
bibliographic_data.abstract: In recent years, federated learning (FL) has emerged as an important distributed machine learning paradigm to collaboratively learn a global model with multiple clients while keeping data local and private. However, the key assumption in most existing works on FL algorithms' convergence analysis is that the noise in stochastic first-order information has a finite variance. This assumption fails for many fat-tailed noise distributions (i.e., 'heavier-tailed' with potentially infinite variance) that have been empirically observed in the FL literature. To address this, we propose an algorithmic framework called FAT-Clipping (federated averaging with two-sided learning rates and clipping), which contains two variants: FAT-Clipping per-round (FAT-Clipping-PR) and FAT-Clipping per-iteration (FAT-Clipping-PI). We show that both variants achieve O((mT)^{2−α/α}) and O((mT)^{(1−α)/(3α−2)}) convergence rates in the strongly-convex and general non-convex settings, respectively. FAT-Clipping-PI further enjoys a linear speedup effect with respect to the number of local updates at each client and is lower-bound matching (i.e., order-optimal).

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes FAT-Clipping, an algorithmic framework for federated learning that addresses the challenges posed by fat-tailed noise.
core_content.key_methodology: FAT-Clipping uses a clipping technique to mitigate the impact of fat-tailed noise and employs a two-sided learning rate mechanism to reduce communication complexity. It contains two variants: FAT-Clipping per-round (FAT-Clipping-PR) and FAT-Clipping per-iteration (FAT-Clipping-PI).
outcomes: Both FAT-Clipping variants achieve specific convergence rates in strongly-convex and general non-convex settings.
FAT-Clipping-PI achieves a linear speedup effect with respect to the number of local updates at each client.
core_content.full_text_markdown: In recent years, federated learning (FL) has emerged as an important distributed machine learning paradigm where clients collaboratively learn a global model while keeping their training data private. However, most existing FL algorithms assume that noise in stochastic first-order information has finite variance, which fails for fat-tailed distributions observed in practice. This paper proposes FAT-Clipping, an algorithmic framework with two variants: FAT-Clipping per-round (FAT-Clipping-PR) and FAT-Clipping per-iteration (FAT-Clipping-PI). Both variants achieve specific convergence rates under different settings and FAT-Clipping-PI provides a linear speedup effect.
